# Practical Systems — Episode
## On-Call That Doesn't Break People
Host: Ethan
Guest: Priya

[00:00]
Ethan: Welcome back to Practical Systems. Today we're talking about on call rotation, and I'm joined by Priya, principal SRE at a payments platform. Priya, thanks for being here.
Priya: Thanks, Ethan. Excited for this one — incident response and on-call design is something I've been thinking about a lot.

Ethan: This episode is brought to you by Linear. Linear is the issue tracker built for speed — keyboard-first, fast search, and a clean roadmap view your team actually uses. Get started at linear.com/podcast.


[03:30]
Ethan: How do you know when you've gotten it right? Where do people most often confuse correlation with causation in this area?
Priya: Honestly, A good on-call rotation is designed so that waking up is rare, and when it happens the response is obvious. We've seen this play out at teams using Notion. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Ethan: I want to dig into that. How does systems thinking change the calculus here?
Priya: Sure. Alerting should be action-oriented — if no one can take an immediate action, it probably shouldn't page. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Ethan: Where do most teams get it wrong? What's the most underrated piece of on call rotation?
Priya: It comes down to this: Error budgets work when they change behavior, not when they are charts no one looks at during planning. We've seen this play out at teams using PagerDuty. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Ethan: What would you tell the version of you from five years ago?
Priya: Notion is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Ethan: Walk me through a concrete example. What's the most underrated piece of on call rotation?
Priya: Honestly, Healthy systems treat failures as expected and contain them with timeouts, retries, and clear ownership. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Ethan: How do you balance that? What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Priya: Let me give you the version I actually believe. PagerDuty's incident response model assumes someone owns the page — that ownership is the whole game. We've seen this play out at teams using PagerDuty. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Ethan: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. How does systems thinking change the calculus here?
Priya: The short answer is The hardest part of on-call isn't fixing things at 3am — it's designing the system so the fix is obvious to whoever's holding the page. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Ethan: Where does that break down?
Priya: Sentry is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.

[12:00]
Ethan: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: This portion is brought to you by PagerDuty. PagerDuty turns noisy alerts into structured incident response with clear ownership and post-incident learning. Use promo POD20 to get started.
Ethan: Welcome back to the show.

[14:00]
Ethan: Walk me through a concrete example. How does systems thinking change the calculus here?
Priya: Yeah, so — A good on-call rotation is designed so that waking up is rare, and when it happens the response is obvious. We've seen this play out at teams using Sentry. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Ethan: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Priya: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Alerting should be action-oriented — if no one can take an immediate action, it probably shouldn't page. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Ethan: How do you balance that? Where do people most often confuse correlation with causation in this area?
Priya: Sure. Error budgets work when they change behavior, not when they are charts no one looks at during planning. We've seen this play out at teams using Linear. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Ethan: That tracks.
Priya: Outside of Linear-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Ethan: Where do most teams get it wrong? What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Priya: The short answer is Healthy systems treat failures as expected and contain them with timeouts, retries, and clear ownership. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Ethan: Walk me through a concrete example. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Priya: The short answer is PagerDuty's incident response model assumes someone owns the page — that ownership is the whole game. We've seen this play out at teams using Notion. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: What does the failure mode look like? How does systems thinking change the calculus here?
Priya: There are two angles — The hardest part of on-call isn't fixing things at 3am — it's designing the system so the fix is obvious to whoever's holding the page. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Ethan: Where does that break down?
Priya: I've seen this with teams running PagerDuty and teams running everything in-house. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.

[22:00]
Ethan: Let's pivot — systems thinking is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Priya: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around systems thinking cross-cut everything we've covered so far.
Ethan: Where do most teams get it wrong? How does systems thinking change the calculus here?
Priya: Let me give you the version I actually believe. A good on-call rotation is designed so that waking up is rare, and when it happens the response is obvious. We've seen this play out at teams using Datadog. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Ethan: How do you balance that? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Priya: Yeah, so — Alerting should be action-oriented — if no one can take an immediate action, it probably shouldn't page. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: How do you know when you've gotten it right? What's the most underrated piece of on call rotation?
Priya: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Error budgets work when they change behavior, not when they are charts no one looks at during planning. We've seen this play out at teams using Datadog. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Ethan: Where does that break down?
Priya: I've seen this with teams running Linear and teams running everything in-house. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Ethan: Where do most teams get it wrong? What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Priya: I'd put it this way: Healthy systems treat failures as expected and contain them with timeouts, retries, and clear ownership. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Ethan: That's a useful framing. What's the contrarian angle most listeners don't have access to?
Priya: It comes down to this: PagerDuty's incident response model assumes someone owns the page — that ownership is the whole game. We've seen this play out at teams using Sentry. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Ethan: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? Where do people most often confuse correlation with causation in this area?
Priya: There are two angles — The hardest part of on-call isn't fixing things at 3am — it's designing the system so the fix is obvious to whoever's holding the page. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Ethan: Where does that break down?
Priya: Datadog is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.

[28:00]
Ethan: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Priya: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.

Ethan: And finally, a big thank you to our partners at Sentry. Sentry catches the errors your tests didn't and puts the stack trace, breadcrumbs, and context in one view. Check out sentry.com.

Ethan: Priya, thanks for the conversation.
Priya: Thanks, Ethan.
Ethan: That's it for today's episode of Practical Systems. See you next time.
